title: Forwarded port hijack from stopped container
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

When the container with automatically assigned forwarded ports is stopped, its ports can be hijacked by other container by automatic assign.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Run the rootful container with options `-p 80 --network slirp4netns:port_handler=slirp4netns` (80 can be replaced to a comma-separated set of ports)

2. Stop that container

3. Run another container with the options from (1)

4. Repeat until pair of containers will get the same host port

5. Try to start that containers and get error `error from slirp4netns while setting up port redirection: map[desc:bad request: add_hostfwd: slirp_add_hostfwd failed]` on the second one startup

**Describe the results you received:**
The host port, used in stopped container is being assigned to newly created container
```
# podman ps -a | grep 36116
2e097abed17b  centos:7.5                         /lib/systemd/syst...  3 days ago    Exited (0) 3 days ago     0.0.0.0:39238->xx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:42174->xxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:44402->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:45526->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:33314->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:41362-33226->xxxx-xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:33769->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:45718-46159->xxxx-xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:41259-45065->xxxx-xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:34934->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:35525->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:41440->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:34893->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:46542->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:36439->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:36116->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:40304->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:34874->xxxxx/tcp  ga-1732
03baeacf40a1  localhost/master:latest            /lib/systemd/syst...  2 days ago    Up 2 days ago             0.0.0.0:36484->xx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:42826->xxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:40884->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:41997->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:43376->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:44808-43912->xxxx-xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:39315->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:43720-41682->xxxx-xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:38973-46771->xxxx-xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:43697->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:42196->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:43364->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:41039->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:36116->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:44084->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:44230->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:36740->xxxx/tcp, 0.0.0.0:45143->xxxxx/tcp  staging
```

**Describe the results you expected:**
New containers should not allocate host ports, already allocated by stopped ones

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

Due to random nature of the automatic host port assignment, the issue occurs rarely. But it makes unusable the stopped container until the container that hijacked the host port is stopped

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
# podman version
Version:      3.1.2
API Version:  3.1.2
Go Version:   go1.15.5
Git Commit:   c464a2f4f36db3b45743f1bb06e7d9549c282685
Built:        Fri Apr 30 10:21:08 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

```

comments.body:
This one would be relatively simple to fix - read all active containers, get their port assignments, ensure we don't conflict - but I have serious reservations about the performance implications of that. It adds a massive amount of database traffic and computation to container creation.

There are 64000 non-privileged ports on the host, so the odds of a collision should be very low under ordinary circumstances. Are you using a large number of containers, or forwarding a very large number of ports (1000+) to each container?
My typical usecase is 15-20 containers with 26 ports forwarded on each. I encountered the issue several times already
This does seem like something we should avoid.  If it proves to be a database issue, could we add a new table of used ports?

Has the potential to be a rather large table, but it is a possibility.

On Tue, May 4, 2021 at 3:29 PM Daniel J Walsh ***@***.***> wrote:
>
> This does seem like something we should avoid. If it proves to be a database issue, could we add a new table of used ports?
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you commented.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub, or unsubscribe.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@mheon @xomachine What should we do with this issue?
Future feature. Probably not going to make 4.0. Maybe 4.1?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@vrothberg can you take a look at this?
@AkihiroSuda @giuseppe is this something slirp can do with some relative performant check?
We can't just do slirp, this also applies to root - needs to be in Podman itself.

> @vrothberg can you take a look at this?

I am busy with SIF but can have a look as soon as I find time.

I had a look. The code has changed substantially in the meantime reducing the risk of a collision. However, if we _really_ want to make sure that a given host port is assigned only once to a container, all of this needs to happen _inside_ libpod and locked to avoid a race condition on one seemingly open port.

@Luap99 can you confirm?
FWIW, I created 600 containers with 15 ports and didn't hit the issue.

The correct solution to this is to not assign host ports on container creation, they should only be assigned at runtime meaning every time you restart the container you get new host ports. Docker does this btw!

However doing this will be super complicated for us, we would need to store ports with host port 0/empty and then when we start the container assign free ones from the host. 
Ideally we would pass 0 as port to the bind call so the kernel could handle this for us but then we would need a way to get the actual ports from slirp4netns, rootlesskit and the rootfull ports so we can store them in the db in order to display them in podman port/ps/inspect.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@Luap99 @vrothberg What should we do with this issue?
I guess keep it open but the fix is complicated and I think there are many items with higher priority
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

We're facing this issue very regularly with a workflow involving creating a set of containers and starting them later. I made a simple Bash script to reproduce the issue, it fails consistently with both Podman 4.9.4 and 5.4.2.

```sh
set -e

image=alpine
network=bug-test-network

podman pull "$image" &>/dev/null
podman network create "$network" >/dev/null

cleanup() {
    podman container prune -f
    podman network prune -f
}
trap cleanup EXIT

for testnum in {1..10}
do
    handles=()
    for containernum in {1..10}
    do
        handle=$(podman create \
            --name "bug_test_container_${containernum}" \
            --network "$network" \
            --rm \
            --publish 12340 \
            --publish 12341 \
            --publish 12342 \
            --publish 12343 \
            --publish 12344 \
            "$image" \
            sleep 5)
        handles=("${handles[@]}" "$handle")
    done

    podman ps -a

    for handle in "${handles[@]}"
    do
        podman start "$handle" >/dev/null
    done
    for handle in "${handles[@]}"
    do
        podman wait "$handle" &>/dev/null || true
    done

    echo "Test $testnum is SUCCESS"
done
```

Just for the record I'm seeing this bug in rootless containers too: `Error: rootlessport listen tcp 0.0.0.0:32803: bind: address already in use` (the failing command being `podman run --detach --rm --publish 8443 --network=bridge docker.io/nitrokey/nethsm:c16fe4ed`).

As others mentioned, it happens when using high concurrency (in tests, in our scenario). Until this is resolved I'm using lower concurrency setting as a workaround.

Thanks for your time! 👋
